claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period; Child has started an apprenticeship in England
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

Firstly, could you please tell me how many children you're looking to claim Child Benefit for, and what are their names and ages?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Thanks for helping me with this. I'm looking to claim for my son Alex. He's 17 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Since Alex is 17 years old, I need to ask a few more questions to determine your eligibility.

Is Alex currently in approved education (such as A-levels, Scottish Highers, or NVQs up to Level 3) or approved training (such as a traineeship or foundation apprenticeship)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he's just started an apprenticeship actually. He's in training to be an electrician.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. I need to clarify a bit more about Alex's apprenticeship, as this is important for determining eligibility.

Is Alex's apprenticeship in England? And is it a paid apprenticeship (where he has a contract of employment and receives a wage)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, it's in England. And yes, it's a paid apprenticeship - he has a proper contract and gets paid.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Unfortunately, this is important information for your eligibility.

I have a couple more questions to confirm your circumstances:

1. Do you live in the UK?
2. Does Alex live with you, or do you contribute towards his upkeep?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - we're in Manchester. And yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I'm sorry to say that based on what you've told me, **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why:

**For Alex (age 17):**
Although you live in the UK and Alex lives with you (which would normally qualify you), the rules state that Child Benefit stops when a child starts an **apprenticeship in England**. Since Alex has begun a paid apprenticeship as an electrician in England with a contract of employment, you would not be eligible to claim Child Benefit for him.

If Alex's circumstances change in the future (for example, if he leaves the apprenticeship and returns to approved education or training), you may become eligible again at that point.

If you have any questions or believe there are other circumstances I should consider, please let me know. I'm happy to help further.

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and has started a paid apprenticeship in England with a contract of employment. According to the Child Benefit rules, you will stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts an apprenticeship in England. This applies regardless of whether the child lives with you or whether you live in the UK. Since Alex has begun a formal, paid apprenticeship in England, you are no longer eligible for Child Benefit for him.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK and Alex lives with them, which would normally meet the basic eligibility criteria. However, Alex (aged 17) has started a paid apprenticeship in England with a contract of employment. Under the Child Benefit rules, eligibility stops when a child starts an apprenticeship in England, regardless of other circumstances.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 17 years old and has started a paid apprenticeship in England with a contract of employment. According to the Child Benefit rules, you will stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts an apprenticeship in England. This applies regardless of whether the child lives with you or whether you live in the UK. Since Alex has begun a formal, paid apprenticeship in England, you are no longer eligible for Child Benefit for him.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK and Alex lives with them, which would normally meet the basic eligibility criteria. However, Alex (aged 17) has started a paid apprenticeship in England with a contract of employment. Under the Child Benefit rules, eligibility stops when a child starts an apprenticeship in England, regardless of other circumstances.
